No Poverty.reason: The course's focus on 'Environmental Justice' and 'Resilience' (Week 11, 13) implicitly addresses the vulnerabilities of the poor to environmental shocks, aligning with Target 1.5.

Zero Hunger.reason: The 'post-disaster Fukushima fisheries' case study (Week 13) offers a tangential link to sustainable food production systems and resilience, relevant to Target 2.4.

Good Health and Well-being.reason: No explicit or strong inferred evidence in the syllabus directly addresses health and well-being targets.

Quality Education.reason: The course explicitly aims to provide knowledge on 'sustainability,' 'climate change,' and 'environmental politics,' directly fulfilling Target 4.7 on education for sustainable development.

Gender Equality.reason: No explicit or strong inferred evidence in the syllabus directly addresses gender equality.

Clean Water and Sanitation.reason: No explicit or strong inferred evidence in the syllabus directly addresses clean water and sanitation.

Affordable and Clean Energy.reason: The course explicitly discusses 'low-carbon transition' and uses 'Wind farms' and 'Energy landscape' as case studies, directly supporting renewable energy and clean technology (Target 7.2).

Decent Work and Economic Growth.reason: Links to decoupling economic growth from environmental degradation (Target 8.4) through 'sustainability' and to sustainable tourism (Target 8.9) via 'rural tourism experience' (Week 3).

Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure.reason: The course covers 'Grassroots innovations for sustainable development' and 'low-carbon transition,' directly implying upgrades to infrastructure and industry for sustainability (Target 9.4).

Reduced Inequalities.reason: A dedicated week on 'Environmental Justice and Pluralism' (Week 11) directly addresses social inclusion and reducing inequalities in environmental outcomes, aligning with Targets 10.2 and 10.3.

Sustainable Cities and Communities.reason: Exploration of 'Resilience and Nature-based Solutions' (Week 13) and 'Energy landscape' (Week 4) connects to creating resilient settlements and regional planning (Targets 11.a, 11.b).

Responsible Consumption and Production.reason: The course's core focus on 'sustainability' and the 'Anthropocene' promotes awareness for sustainable lifestyles and efficient resource use, aligning with Targets 12.2 and 12.8.

Climate Action.reason: The course is fundamentally about the 'politics of Climate Change' and the 'Anthropocene,' directly addressing climate policy, awareness, and adaptation (Targets 13.2, 13.3). This is a central theme.

Life Below Water.reason: Discussions on 'Offshore Wind in the North Sea' (Week 5) and 'post-disaster Fukushima fisheries' (Week 13) directly engage with marine ecosystems and their management (Targets 14.2, 14.4).

Life on Land.reason: Explicit coverage of 'Reconstituting Nature Conservation' (Week 2) and 'Rewilding, restoring and reconnecting with nature' (Week 6) directly addresses the conservation and restoration of terrestrial ecosystems (Targets 15.1, 15.5).

Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions.reason: The course's title 'Anthropocene and Geopolitics' and explicit focus on 'Environmental Governance and Politics' (Week 10) and 'Environmental Justice' (Week 11) make this a core theme, aligning with Targets 16.3, 16.6, 16.7.

Partnerships for the Goals.reason: The course explores 'Globalised Environmental Knowledge' (Week 8) and 'Citizen Science and Participatory Knowledge' (Week 12), which are key to enhancing global cooperation and multi-stakeholder partnerships (Targets 17.6, 17.17).
